schools.summary: Chrysostom commends Acts as little known yet as profitable as the Gospels, rich in doctrine of the Holy Ghost, showing the Gospels' predictions fulfilled and the disciples transformed by the Spirit; the 'former treatise' Luke's Gospel (to Theophilus); the pregnant 'began to do and teach' — the Gospel what Jesus began, the Acts what the risen Christ continues by his Spirit through the apostles, the living Lord still working.

themes: all that Jesus began to do and teach
the living Christ by the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the Gospel reached 'until the day he was taken up' (the ascension the hinge); Lightfoot weighs the syntax of 'through the Holy Ghost,' taking it (with the Greek order and most translations) with the giving of commandments — Christ giving his parting commands by the Spirit (as God dictated to the prophets), to the chosen apostles; the apostolic mission grounded in Christ's authority and the Spirit's working.

debates.question: Does 'through the Holy Ghost' attach to the choosing or the commanding?

sides.position: To the commanding — 'he through the Holy Ghost had given commandments' (the Greek order, most translations)

themes: until the day he was taken up
through the Holy Ghost gave commandments

schools.summary: Gill: the certainty of Christ's death (confirmed by the pierced side, the centurion, the burial) and his risen life ('he raised himself,' declared the Son of God with power, Romans 1:4); the 'many infallible proofs' — repeated, undeniable appearances over forty days, beyond all doubt; Christ using the forty days to teach 'the things pertaining to the kingdom of God' (its spiritual nature), preparing the apostles for their mission.

themes: shewed himself alive by many infallible proofs
speaking of the kingdom of God

schools.summary: Lightfoot: 'assembled together' (synalizomenos) from 'assembly' (a gathering), though some render 'eating with them'; Gill: the last meeting at Bethany/Olivet; Christ commands the apostles not to depart Jerusalem but to wait for 'the promise of the Father' (the Holy Spirit, promised by the Father and by Christ, John 14-16) — the duty of waiting for the Spirit's power before the work (Luke 24:49), resting on the sure promise.

themes: wait for the promise of the Father

schools.summary: Gill: John's baptism water immersion (he the first administrator), these Christ's own words (not in the evangelists); Lightfoot stresses 'not many days hence' (pointing to Pentecost ten days off); the contrast of John's outward water baptism with the inward, empowering baptism of the Holy Ghost (the substance the water pointed to, Matthew 3:11) — the surpassing Pentecostal gift, near at hand.

themes: the surpassing baptism of the Spirit

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the disciples eager for freedom and relief after their perils, asking not 'when' but 'whether at this time' — still hoping for the restoration; the persistence of their earthly, national expectation (David's throne, Israel freed) even after the resurrection and forty days of kingdom-teaching; the slowness of good men to lay aside cherished mistaken hopes, which Christ corrects gently by redirecting them to their true mission.

themes: wilt thou restore the kingdom to Israel
slowness to lay aside mistaken hopes

schools.summary: Barnes: Christ's answer meets their inquiry — the time of God's kingdom not for them to know ('of that day knoweth no man,' Matthew 24:36), God reproving vain curiosity (1 Thessalonians 5:1; 2 Peter 3:10); 'times' any indefinite period, 'seasons' a fixed time, all reserved to the Father's own power; the believer to cease speculation, leave the times in God's hand, and attend to duty and watchfulness.

themes: it is not for you to know the times
the Father hath put in his own power

schools.summary: Gill: the power the Holy Ghost's — strength to preach, work miracles, and courage amid persecution (not the worldly ease they sought); the threefold promise — the Spirit's power, the office of witness to Christ, and the expanding field (Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the uttermost earth) the very outline of Acts; the universal commission to all nations, the gospel advancing in widening circles by the Spirit's power.

themes: witnesses unto me in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria
unto the uttermost part of the earth

schools.summary: Barnes: the disciples did not see Christ rise (the resurrection better proved by seeing him alive after), but the ascension could only be confirmed by their seeing it — so Christ ascended in open day, visibly, while they beheld; the cloud (token of the divine glory) receiving him out of sight; the bodily ascension of the risen Christ to heaven, attested by the apostles' eyewitness, the foundation of his heavenly reign.

themes: while they beheld, he was taken up

schools.summary: Barnes: 'looked steadfastly' = gazed intently (as Luke 4:20), the earnest look of those amazed, longing to catch a last glimpse of their Master; Gill: they kept looking up after the cloud hid him; the 'two men in white apparel' angels (the white betokening heavenly purity, as at the resurrection, Luke 24:4) sent to the bewildered disciples — the ministry of angels attending Christ's ascension as his whole work, God's grace instructing them.

Gill: 'ye men of Galilee' no reproach but recognition and a token of distinguishing grace; Barnes: a slight censure calling them from vain gazing to duty; the great promise — 'this same Jesus shall so come in like manner' — the second coming bodily, visibly, in clouds, personally, as he ascended (Revelation 1:7); the ascension the pattern of the return, the disciples turned from idle longing to faithful hope and service.

themes: this same Jesus shall so come in like manner
the promise of the second coming

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the disciples could return in peace only because the angel referred them to another coming (the promise consoling the parting), and infers from 'a sabbath day's journey' that the ascension was on a sabbath; the apostles obeying the charge to return to Jerusalem and wait (v.4); the precise note of distance (Olivet ~three-quarters of a mile) a mark of historical exactness — obedient waiting sustained by the hope of Christ's return.

Chrysostom notes the order of names — Peter, James, John now the leading three — and that Luke does well to list the disciples, for since one had betrayed Christ it was fitting to record the faithful eleven; Gill discusses the upper room (private house or temple); the persevering apostles (restored after their flight and Peter's denial) gathered to wait and pray, the roll of the faithful set against the traitor's fall — the nucleus of the Church.

themes: they went up into an upper room

schools.summary: Gill: they prayed for the promised Spirit, their preservation and qualification, and the comfort of their hearts — unanimous, constant, assiduous, importunate; the gathered company (apostles, the faithful women, Mary the mother of Jesus, Christ's now-believing brethren, John 7:5) united in prayer; the Spirit came at Pentecost on a praying Church — prayer the preparation for power; Mary named as a worshipper among the disciples, not above them.

themes: continued with one accord in prayer
the women, and Mary the mother of Jesus

schools.summary: Gill: Peter rose as the forward first-mover, zealous for Christ whom he had lately denied (eager to show his restored devotion), the senior and apostle of the circumcision; Matthew Henry: Judas's sin made a vacancy in the apostolic college (ordained twelve for the twelve tribes), to be filled; the restored Peter (pardoned and recommissioned, John 21) taking leadership of the hundred and twenty, the nucleus of the Church before Pentecost.

themes: Peter stood up in the midst

schools.summary: Barnes: 'Men and brethren' a customary address of affection and respect, the prophecy Psalm 41:9 (applied to Judas by Christ, John 13:18); Peter teaches the betrayal of Judas foretold by the Holy Ghost through David and 'must needs' be fulfilled — the inspiration of Scripture (the Spirit speaking through the human author, 2 Peter 1:21), its certain fulfilment, the betrayal foretold yet leaving Judas's guilt (Luke 22:22), the providence overruling his sin.

themes: the Holy Ghost by the mouth of David
this scripture must needs be fulfilled

schools.summary: Barnes: 'numbered with us' means reckoned among the apostles, not a true Christian — Jesus knew he never loved him, declared him a devil (John 6:70), knew his character when he chose him; Judas held the highest office and shared the ministry yet was a false disciple, the warning that office is no proof of grace (Matthew 7:21); the highest privilege abused ending in the deepest ruin, the need to examine our profession.

Gill treats vv.18-19 as Luke's parenthesis; the purchase harmonized with Matthew 27:7 (Judas's returned money bought the field, the priests buying it in his name); the death harmonized with Matthew 27:5 (Judas hanged himself, then the rope/branch breaking, fell headlong and burst) — the two accounts supplementing not contradicting; the fearful judgment on the traitor, the wages of betraying Christ ('the reward of iniquity'), the field of blood a memorial of his crime.

debates.question: How are Acts and Matthew reconciled on Judas's death and the field?

sides.position: Judas hanged himself (Matthew), then fell and burst (Acts); his returned money bought the field (the priests acting in his name)

themes: purchased a field with the reward of iniquity

schools.summary: Barnes: 'Aceldama' Syro-Chaldaic for 'the field of blood,' preserving the memory of Judas's guilt (bought with innocent blood, Matthew 27:8); Clarke: the whole affair (Judas's remorse, his testimony to Christ's innocence, his death) publicly known in Jerusalem; the providence making the traitor's crime a standing public memorial, confirming the gospel facts (all Jerusalem knowing them) and warning against betraying Christ for gain.

themes: known to all the dwellers at Jerusalem
the public memorial of the crime

schools.summary: Barnes: the first citation from Psalm 69:25 ('let his habitation be desolate'), the second from Psalm 109:8 ('let another take his office'), the plural changed to singular and applied to Judas; Peter grounds the filling of Judas's place on Scripture — the Psalms foretelling the traitor's desolation and the transfer of his 'bishoprick' (apostolic office) to another; the Church acting on scriptural warrant, the providence foretelling even the fall and replacement.

Gill: the choice of one among the men (not women), from among themselves, known for ability, integrity, wisdom, and holy conversation — not a stranger; the first qualification (Peter's) being long companionship with the apostles and Christ throughout his ministry, an eyewitness of all he did, since the apostle's office was to witness; the prudence of choosing a tried and known character for the high office.

themes: all the time the Lord went in and out

schools.summary: Clarke: 'beginning from the baptism of John' = from Christ's baptism in Jordan, when his ministry properly began; Gill: the candidate's faith and firmness tried by following from that beginning; the apostle must witness Christ's whole ministry (baptism to ascension) and above all be 'a witness of his resurrection' — the central apostolic testimony (Acts 2:32), grounded in having seen the risen Lord, the foundation of the gospel.

themes: beginning from the baptism of John
a witness with us of his resurrection

schools.summary: Gill: Peter's motion approved by the whole company, who proposed two by name (the choice by the whole assembly, not the apostles alone); Lightfoot on 'Joseph called Barsabas' (Jose/Joseph one name); the two qualified candidates — Joseph Barsabas (surnamed Justus) and Matthias — nominated, the choice narrowed to two then left to God; the orderly, communal, prayerful process of filling the vacancy, both candidates worthy.

Barnes: unable to agree, they invoked God's direction (an example for choosing those in sacred office); 'which knowest the hearts of all men' the peculiar prerogative of God (Jeremiah 17:10), yet ascribed expressly to Christ (Revelation 2:23; John 2:25) — so the prayer may be to Christ, ascribing him the divine omniscience (a witness to his deity); the choice of an apostle, requiring discernment of the heart, rightly left to the heart-knower (1 Samuel 16:7).

debates.question: Is the prayer to the 'heart-knowing Lord' addressed to God the Father or to Christ?

sides.position: May be to Christ — the heart-searcher (Revelation 2:23; John 2:25) who chose the original apostles, ascribing him divine omniscience
To God, whose prerogative it is to know all hearts (Jeremiah 17:10)

themes: Thou which knowest the hearts of all men
shew whether of these two thou hast chosen

schools.summary: Gill: the ministry and apostleship (preaching, ordinances, planting churches, miracles) Judas held and lost; 'by transgression fell' — the office forfeited by his own sin; Lightfoot expounds 'his own place' by rabbinic usage (Balaam and the wicked 'went to his own place, that is, hell,' appointed for idolaters and betrayers) — Judas going to the place his sin fitted him for; the warning that every man goes at last to his own place.

debates.question: What is 'his own place' that Judas went to?

sides.position: The place of the lost (hell), the doom his betrayal fitted him for

themes: from which Judas by transgression fell
that he might go to his own place

schools.summary: Gill: the method of the lot (names cast and drawn, 'balloting,' likened to the day of atonement lots, Leviticus 16:8); the lot an OT means of seeking God's decision ('the whole disposing is of the LORD,' Proverbs 16:33), used before Pentecost (some noting the Church did not use it after the Spirit came, others defending it as a prayerful seeking of God's will); Matthias chosen by God's decision, numbered with the eleven — the apostolic twelve restored before the outpouring.

debates.question: Was the use of the lot to choose Matthias proper?

sides.position: Yes — a legitimate, prayerful OT means of seeking God's decision before the Spirit came (Proverbs 16:33)
Noted that the Church did not use the lot after Pentecost, being then guided by the Spirit
